ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean fish beehoon soup; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle soup dish' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (fish soup with noodles) used as the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for Singaporean double fish beehoon soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of double fish beehoon soup (no milk) is approximately 500 g, comprising broth, a double portion of fish slices (both fresh and deep-fried), rice vermicelli, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Double Fish Beehoon Soup (No Milk) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 'Double fish beehoon soup (no milk)', CR ID F05060043. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to mixed fish beehoon soup
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for double fish beehoon soup (no milk). GI estimated at 53 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (beehoon), which reports a GI of approximately 53 (low). The soup matrix (broth, protein from fish, fibre from vegetables) further moderates postprandial glucose response relative to plain rice vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (9.68 g/100g × 500 g serving = 48.4 g) / 100 = 25.7 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 302 mg/100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1510 mg sodium, approaching the daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth and soy sauce, avoid drinking the full soup, and limit portion size to manage sodium intake, as a full bowl can contribute up to 75% of the recommended daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) is a refined carbohydrate with a moderate GI (~53); a full bowl provides approximately 48 g available carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion or substitute with additional vegetables, avoid sweet sauces, and rely on the double fish protein content to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.4 g/100 g) and fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) support cardiovascular health; however, sodium at 302 mg/100 g warrants caution.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is broadly suitable for cardiovascular disease management due to its low saturated fat and omega-3 content from fish, but patients should minimise broth consumption to limit sodium intake and reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with evidence-based anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low at ~0.4 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content from fish; request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium, which can exacerbate tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Fish Beehoon Soup (No Milk) is a Singaporean hawker-style noodle soup dish consisting of a double portion of fish slices — both fresh (poached) and deep-fried — served with thick rice vermicelli (beehoon) and leafy vegetables in a clear, savoury fish broth. The broth is typically seasoned with ginger, light soy sauce, and white pepper, giving it a clean, mildly umami flavour. The fresh fish slices are tender and delicate, while the deep-fried pieces add a contrasting crisp texture. The dish is dairy-free by specification (no evaporated milk added), distinguishing it from the milk-based variant common at Singaporean fish soup stalls. Nutritionally, it provides 59 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (2.7 g/100 g) from the double fish portion, low fat (1.71 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (9.68 g/100 g) from the rice vermicelli. Sodium is borderline at 302 mg/100 g, making broth management important for hypertensive individuals. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular and joint health. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli data, with the protein and fibre matrix further moderating glucose response. This dish is a popular lunch option in Singapore hawker centres and food courts, commonly ordered by health-conscious diners seeking a lighter, lower-calorie noodle soup.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F05060043) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact — the HPB entry corresponds precisely to this dish name and preparation variant.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F05060043 as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.4 g/100 g based on typical fish and frying oil composition for this dish type; this is an approximation as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under 'western' cuisine and applied dietary tags including 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based', which are factually incorrect for a fish-containing dish; these have been corrected to 'Singaporean' cuisine with appropriate dietary flags. Cultural tags have been corrected accordingly.
